name: Rope and Cordage
tagline: Twisted and braided fibres that turn tension into reliable force transmission, binding, and lifting
problem:
Many critical tasks require pulling forces larger than a bare hand can apply, or holding forces steady for long periods: dragging carcasses, hoisting bundles, lashing loads to frames, making nets, climbing safely, and binding tool heads to handles. Vines snap unpredictably; green bark slips; short grass bundles fatigue. The problem is scalable tensile members: materials that share load across many parallel fibres locked together by twist or braid so partial breaks do not instantly cascade.

Cordage also stores technique: a well-made rope is a composite whose strength emerges from friction between plies and the helix angle distributing strain. Without understanding twist direction (S and Z) and balance, ropes untwist under load or kink into useless hockles.

In recovery contexts, cordage is a universal adapter: it connects almost every other primitive technology — shelters, traps, boats, packs, and later pulleys and machines.
overview:
Traditional cordage begins with harvesting fibres: bast from inner bark (lime, willow, nettle, paper mulberry), leaf fibres (agave, yucca), animal sinew, rawhide strips, or hair. Fibres are retted or scraped clean, dried, then split to consistent width. Short fibres are spun into continuous yarn by adding twist — twist traps fibre ends against neighbours via friction.

Yarns are combined into cord by plying: two or more yarns twisted together in the opposite direction from their spin, locking the structure. Three-strand rope is made by twisting three plies into a larger helix. Braids (plaits) distribute abrasion more evenly and reduce twist-induced torque on suspended loads.

Strength scales with cross-sectional area of load-bearing fibres and quality of processing. Kinks, sharp bends over small radii, and UV degradation are dominant failure modes. Storage dry prevents rot; natural ropes stretch when wet and may shrink drying — important when building tension structures.

Mechanically, rope is a flexible tensile element in machines: with a wheel it becomes a belt drive ancestor; with a grooved wheel it becomes a pulley line; with capstans it multiplies human pulling power through friction wraps.

principles.name: Twist balance (S and Z)
principles.explanation: Fibres spun in one direction (Z-twist) must be plied in the opposite direction (S-twist). This 'counter-twist' creates internal friction that prevents the rope from unravelling under tension.

principles.name: Helix Angle and Strength
principles.explanation: The angle of the twist relative to the rope's axis (typically 30-40°) balances flexibility and strength. Steeper angles (more twist) improve grip but reduce maximum tensile strength by putting fibres into shear.

principles.name: Friction sharing
principles.explanation: In twisted rope, fibres press together; load transfers between fibres by friction, so local breaks do not immediately unload the whole cross section.

principles.name: Bend radius and strength
principles.explanation: Fibre outer paths stretch more around tight bends; undersized sheaves cut strength dramatically — a rule pulleys must respect (D/d ratio of at least 8:1 is ideal for natural fibre).

principles.name: Creep and relaxation
principles.explanation: Natural fibres elongate under sustained load; knots slip microscopically. Tensioned assemblies (like bridge cables or tool lashings) need periodic retightening.

components.name: Fibre stock
components.function: Provides long-chain cellulosic or protein strands bearing tension
components.position: Processed into consistent ribbon or bundles before spinning
components.madeFrom: Bast (Nettle, Inner bark), leaf (Agave, Yucca), sinew, hair, or later cotton/hemp
components.criticalNote: Avoid 'brittle' fibres; test by wrapping around a finger — if it snaps, it is unsuitable for cordage

components.name: Spindle or thigh spindle
components.function: Adds controlled twist while drafting fibres into yarn
components.position: Hand-held or rolled along thigh; whorl provides momentum
components.madeFrom: Wood, bone, or fired clay whorl weight

components.name: Plied cord or rope
components.function: Bears load in tension; ties, lifts, and wraps
components.position: Finished product or input to nets and lashings

components.name: Splice or knot interface
components.function: Terminates rope or joins lengths without sharp cutting if possible
components.position: Eyes around posts, bends around timber, hitches to rings
components.madeFrom: Same cordage; wooden fid for thick laid rope later
components.criticalNote: Most knots reduce rated strength by 40-60%; splices (interweaving strands) preserve up to 90% strength

components.name: Use-case boundary
components.function: Defines what Rope and Cordage is meant to accomplish and what it must not be used for
components.madeFrom: Written requirements, hazard notes, local constraints, and responsible operator assignment
components.criticalNote: A vague use case leads to unsafe improvisation.

rawMaterials.name: Bast fibre plants
rawMaterials.purpose: High tensile vegetable cordage without metal tools
earthLocations: Lime family (Tilia) in Eurasia; Nettle (Urtica) globally; Dogbane (Apocynum) in North America; Paper Mulberry (Broussonetia) in Pacific
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Cellulose-rich biomass from recycled hydroponic stems; microbial cellulose (SCOBY) grown in vats can be dried and twisted into emergency cordage.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Retting (submerging in water for 7-14 days to dissolve pectins), rinsing, stripping ribbons, drying

rawMaterials.name: Leaf fibres (Hard fibres)
rawMaterials.purpose: Hard-wearing, rot-resistant cord for nets and dragging tasks
earthLocations: Agave/Sisal (Americas), New Zealand flax (Phormium), Yucca (Arid regions)
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Extruded polymer fibres (Nylon/Polyester) from plastic waste; carbon fibre tow when high-tech looms are available.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Scraping pulp (decortication), sun drying, sometimes beating to separate strands

rawMaterials.name: Animal sinew
rawMaterials.purpose: Extremely strong fine cord for traps, bowstrings, and tool backing
earthLocations: Tendons from large herbivores (Deer, Elk, Cow)
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Clean tendons, dry until hard, pound to separate into ribbons, twist wet then dry under tension

buildSteps.title: Harvest and retting
buildSteps.description: Cut stems before they go to seed. Submerge in water (pond retting) or leave on grass (dew retting). Bacteria consume the glues holding the bark to the core. Stop when ribbons peel away easily.
buildSteps.warningNote: Retting water contains concentrated organic acids and bacteria; avoid contact with open wounds and work in ventilated areas

buildSteps.title: Drafting and spinning
buildSteps.description: Clean the retted ribbons of any remaining pulp. Pull (draft) a small bundle of fibres to the desired thickness. Introduce a Z-twist (clockwise) by rolling between palms or using a drop spindle.
buildSteps.warningNote: Keep thickness consistent; thin spots (slubs) are where the rope will eventually fail

buildSteps.title: Plying for stability
buildSteps.description: Take two or more Z-spun yarns. Fold them together and introduce an S-twist (counter-clockwise). The opposing twists will lock against each other, creating a 'balanced' cord that doesn't untwist when released.
buildSteps.warningNote: Apply even tension to both strands during plying to prevent 'cow-tails' (uneven wraps)

buildSteps.title: Scaling to rope
buildSteps.description: For thick rope, ply the plied cords again in the Z-direction. This 'hawser-laid' structure is the basis for heavy lifting lines. For long lengths, use a 'rope walk' — a long clear path where multiple people can twist simultaneously.
buildSteps.warningNote: Heavy tension can store dangerous energy; if a line snaps during the making process, it can whip back

buildSteps.title: Finishing and maintenance
buildSteps.description: Singeth off loose fibre ends over a small flame to reduce abrasion points. Dress with beeswax or pine tar to improve water resistance. Coil loosely to prevent set-kinks.
buildSteps.warningNote: Check for 'crown wear' regularly; if 20% of the surface fibres are fuzzy, the rope is unsafe for life-critical loads

history.event: Impressions of twisted fibres on artefacts suggest advanced cordage predates preserved rope in many regions.
Earliest physical evidence of cordage from burnt clay impressions in Dolní Věstonice.
Domesticated fibre crops (flax, hemp) scale cordage production for agriculture and transport.

impact:
Cordage is infrastructure in string form: it makes packs, nets, traps, sails, and hoists possible. It couples human force to loads and couples components into assemblies — lashings precede nails economically in many environments.

In later engineering, the same tensile logic underlies cables, wire rope, and fibre-reinforced composites. Understanding twist, bend radius, and termination is still how bridges and cranes are kept safe.

For off-world bases, lightweight high-strength cable may be among the first complex products once polymer or carbon fibre processes exist — but early settlements still benefit from vegetable fibre for filters, ties, and soft interfaces.
